zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Richard Harold, I於1250年出生在威爾士布洛可爾哈洛爾德斯頓 韋斯特時，他的父親Sir William Harold20歲，母親Elsbeth Brienne, Princess of Constantinople20歲。他於1269年與Isabel Martin在威爾士布洛可爾哈洛爾德斯頓 韋斯特結婚。他們至少是2個兒子的父母。

nameMeanings.name: Harold,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from the Middle English personal name Harold (Old Norse Haraldr, borrowed from an ancient Germanic form Harjawald). Alternatively, the name may arise from the Middle English personal name Harwold, Harold (Old English Hereweald), particularly in Suffolk, where there was also post-medieval confusion with Hurrell . These all go back to an ancient Germanic personal name composed of the elements heri, hari ‘army’ + wald ‘rule’, which is attested in Europe from an early date; the Roman historian Tacitus records a certain Cariovalda, chief of the ancient Germanic tribe of the Batavi, as early as the 1st century AD See also Herald .
English: habitational name from Harrold (Bedfordshire), from Old English hār ‘gray’ + wald ‘large tract of woodland, high woodland’.

Irish: this name is of direct Norse origin (see 1 above), but is also occasionally a variant of Harrell and Hurrell . Spellings without final d have been adopted as the Anglicized form of the Gaelic Ó hEarghail (see Harrell ).

variants.name: Airoldi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Chantal, Germaine, Herve, Marcel, Rosaire.

Italian: patronymic or plural form of the old personal name Airoldo, a variant of Aroldo, from ancient Germanic Hariwald, composed of the elements hari ‘army’ + wald ‘power, authority’; see Harold . This surname is also found in France, but to French Canada it was nevertheless brought from Italy.

variants.name: Haroldson
meanings: Americanized form of Norwegian Haraldsen and Swedish Haraldsson: patronymic from the personal name Harald, Old Norse Haraldr (see Harold ). Compare Haraldson and Haroldsen .

variants.name: Harrod
meanings: English: variant of   Harold  . Compare   Herod  .

variants.name: Hurrell
meanings: English: nickname presumably for someone with shaggy or unkempt hair, from a diminutive of Old French hure ‘hair’.
Irish (Antrim): possibly an Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEarghail ‘descendant of Earghal’, a variant of Fearghal (see Farrell , Arrell , and Harrell ).

variants.name: Haraldson
meanings: Americanized form (and a rare Swedish variant) of Swedish Haraldsson: patronymic from the personal name Harald, Old Norse Haraldr (see Harold ). This is also an Americanized form of the Norwegian cognate Haraldsen. Compare Haralson , Haroldsen , Haroldson , and Harrelson .

variants.name: Harrold
meanings: Scottish and English: variant of   Harold  .

variants.name: Harral
meanings: English (Yorkshire and Lancashire): variant of   Harold  .

variants.name: Harrell
meanings: Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEarghail ‘descendant of Earghal’, a variant of the personal name Fearghal (see Farrell ).

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Richard Harold, I was born in 1250, in Haroldston West, Pembrokeshire, Wales, his father, Sir William Harold, was 20 and his mother, Elsbeth Brienne, Princess of Constantinople, was 20. He married Isabel Martin in 1269, in Haroldston West, Pembrokeshire, Wales. They were the parents of at least 2 sons.

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Richard Harold, I(이)가 1250년 웨일스 p브로케실르 하로드스턴 웨스트에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir William Harold(은)는 20세였고, 그의 어머니 Elsbeth Brienne, Princess of Constantinople(은)는 20세였다. 그는 1269년 웨일스 p브로케실르 하로드스턴 웨스트에서 Isabel Martin(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들을 두었다.
